title: Import Excel to Subitems
excerpt:
It would be great to be able to import a spreadsheet to sub items.  This would be usefull becuase it allows us to have different columns in the sub items.  For instance… 
I have an item on a board for ordering material, …

posts.author: gknight (Garrett)
posts.cookedHtml:
It would be great to be able to import a spreadsheet to sub items. This would be usefull becuase it allows us to have different columns in the sub items. For instance…

I have an item on a board for ordering material, one of many different things related to an install. I’d like to be able to import our material list as sub items under that so I can check off on what items have been ordered or what items need to be ordered.

posts.author: Ohad (Ohad Zamir)
posts.cookedHtml:
I have a board for managing Professional Services activities.
Each Item is a project and it’s sub items are the detailed activities.
Previously we managed this using sheets, and we want to import al that data into Monday.

Unfortunately there is no convenient way of importing data into subitems.

posts.author: BGBO (Beau )
posts.cookedHtml: We have a similar need, as well. We have clients who would like to update existing sub-item columns from CSV, but they are currently having to enter those individually.

posts.author: kolaai (alfred)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hello @BGBO,

The Toolkit can help you out here.

In the list of boards, select the Subitem board. Subitem boards are named after the main board. So if the main board is called “Board A”, the Subitem board is named as “Subitems of Board A”.
The rest of the process is the same as in the main board. Here is a demo of how to the app works.

If you need any help with the app, you can always reach out at support@kolaai.com

posts.author: kkovriga (Katerina Kovriga)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi guys,

You may try our Smart Spreadsheet for monday app.

You’ll be able to use the Spreadsheet as an Item view and add (import) separate independent spreadsheets to your items and subitems, then work with the data using all the Excel-like features (cell formulas, pivot tables, filtration, conditional formatting, etc.).

Hope it helps your case.

Best regards,
Katerina Kovriga, Stiltsoft

posts.cookedHtml: Definately would like to see this. I made a new board with so many subitems and had to just design it to not have subitems in the end

posts.author: Indrani (Indrani C.)
posts.cookedHtml:
Simply allow

  1. Copy pasting from one table format into Monday format for all columns. (just like it is possible to do for the Item names)
  2. Automate Excel/CSV import directly into a group (and not necessarily create a new board) - therefore remove the extra step of moving to the relevant board after import.
  3. Automate excel/CSV import directly into subitems. (For example under a project item I have some quick secondary google research data to plot, I’d like to be able to put the data readily visible under the main task and be able to assign people to each research outcome). Uplaoding a file isn’t as user friendly when you need such quick research data to be quickly readable, available, actionable. Excel can be used for larger deeper research.

posts.author: allisonbmccoy (Allison McCoy)
posts.cookedHtml: This would be useful for me as well. I have subitems with dates and formulas, and I need to be able to copy or import these in.

posts.author: jamespatten (James Patten)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi All,

I’m happy to say that you can now import an Excel file into subitems using the Data Importer monday app.

excels monday sub items1641×875 116 KB

I’ve also made a tutorial for the step-by-step on how to do this.

Happy to answer any questions on this or take feedback requests!

James

posts.author: cphu (Christine Phu)
posts.cookedHtml: +1 This would be super helpful to have native in Monday for enterprise accounts. It’s not easy for us to use 3rd party apps (has to go through a long security and procurement process)

posts.author: MsquareAutomation (Msquare Automation Solutions)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi @gknight
You can achieve this using Make.

A common approach is to upload the spreadsheet (Excel/CSV) to Google Sheets or another accessible source, and then use a Make scenario to read each row and automatically create subitems under the correct parent item.

The flow would look like this:

  • Make reads each row from the spreadsheet (each row = one material item).

  • It identifies the main item on your board (for example, the install or order request).

  • For each row, Make creates a subitem and fills the columns such as material name, quantity, status, etc.

This allows you to import your full material list as subitems, so you can then track which items are ordered, pending, or completed directly in the board without manually creating each subitem.
